rationale_for_baseline_tier: A collapsed, semi-conscious woman of childbearing age with sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, shoulder-tip pain, amenorrhoea and abnormal bleeding, who is profoundly pale, cold, clammy and barely responsive, is in decompensated (Class III/IV) haemorrhagic shock from a likely ruptured ectopic pregnancy. This is an immediate threat to life measured in minutes — T5 (call 000, emergency ambulance) is the only appropriate tier. Definitive care (resuscitation, transfusion, emergency surgery) is in hospital; the telehealth task is instant escalation plus first-aid coaching.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse with severe abdominal pain and signs of major internal bleeding (pallor, cold clammy skin, drowsiness) in a woman whose period is late
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately — within seconds, before any further history-taking
escalation_edges.rationale: Decompensated haemorrhagic shock is peri-arrest; every minute of delay to resuscitation and surgery increases mortality.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This is an emergency. Call 000 now for an ambulance and tell them she has collapsed with severe abdominal pain and signs of major internal bleeding, and that her period is late. I'll stay with you.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: She vomits or her breathing becomes noisy while drowsy
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately roll her into the recovery position and keep 000 on the line
escalation_edges.rationale: Reduced consciousness plus vomiting risks airway obstruction and aspiration; the recovery position protects the airway until paramedics arrive.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she is sick or her breathing sounds gurgly, gently roll her onto her side into the recovery position and keep the phone on speaker with 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: She becomes completely unresponsive and stops breathing normally
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Start CPR immediately and continue until paramedics take over
escalation_edges.rationale: Haemorrhagic cardiac arrest (PEA) — chest compressions and rapid paramedic arrival are the only chance of survival; the 000 call-taker gives compression coaching.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she stops responding and isn't breathing normally, lay her on her back on a firm surface and start pushing hard and fast in the centre of her chest — the 000 call-taker will count you through it.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: The only person present is a roommate who is frightened and doing all the hands-on care while the patient is barely responsive
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give one clear instruction at a time, keep the roommate on the line, and have them prop the door open and clear access for paramedics; do not overload them with information.
Always treat collapse with pelvic pain in this group as a possible pregnancy-related haemorrhage until excluded — this raises, never lowers, the urgency.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an emergency ambulance right now and say she has collapsed with severe abdominal pain and signs of major internal bleeding and that her period is late. Lay her flat, raise her legs and keep her warm. Give her nothing to eat or drink. Stay with her; if she vomits, roll her onto her side; if she stops breathing normally, start CPR with the call-taker's help.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is a life-threatening emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or a GP appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital and ambulance service will document the resuscitation, imaging, hCG result and any surgery; a discharge summary should follow to the GP for post-operative and contraceptive/fertility follow-up.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as period pain, a simple faint or gastro and advising GP/urgent-care review or watchful waiting instead of an emergency ambulance.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Ongoing intra-abdominal haemorrhage progresses to cardiac arrest and death before definitive surgical control.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising an emergency but advising self-transport to ED rather than a 000 ambulance — a peri-arrest patient could arrest en route without paramedic resuscitation capability.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Over-triage is not a realistic risk here — T5 is correct; any tier at or above T5 is appropriate.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The exact diagnosis (ruptured ectopic vs ruptured ovarian cyst vs other intra-abdominal haemorrhage) cannot be established via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Act on the recognised syndrome — collapse with haemorrhagic shock — and escalate to 000 immediately; the precise cause is a hospital determination.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Delaying escalation to gather more history or attempting to distinguish the causes remotely.
